success: Touch each object as you count.

questions.prompt: Push and pull are ways to make things _____.

passage: We walk inside. We share materials. We listen when a friend is talking.

subject: Social Studies

passage: A map can show where things are. The rug is by the shelf. The door is near the cubbies.

learning_target: I can tell needs and wants apart.

subject: Character
title: The Crayon Choice
prompt: A friend cannot find a blue crayon. What is the kind choice?
answer: Share your blue crayon.
learning_target: I can choose a kind action.

subject: Character
title: The Broken Block Tower
prompt: You bumped the block tower by accident. What should you do?
choices: Tell the truth and help fix it.
answer: Tell the truth and help fix it.

subject: Character
title: Waiting for a Turn
prompt: You want the swing, but another child is using it. What should you do?
choices: Wait or ask for a turn kindly.
answer: Wait or ask for a turn kindly.

subject: Character
title: Clean-Up Bell
prompt: The clean-up bell rings. What helps the class?
answer: Put materials away.

subject: Math
title: Market Math Mystery
prompt: A class sells 128 muffins in the morning and 96 muffins after lunch. They bake 75 more. How many muffins did they have to sell in all?

subject: Math
title: Garden Flag Fractions
prompt: A flag is split into 8 equal stripes. 3 stripes are green and 5 are yellow. What fraction is green?
model_it: The denominator is the number of equal parts. The numerator is the green parts.

subject: Math
title: Tile Patio Challenge
prompt: A patio has 6 rows of tiles with 7 tiles in each row. How many tiles are there?

subject: Math
title: Rain Gauge Data
prompt: Rain fell 3 inches on Monday, 1 inch on Tuesday, and 4 inches on Wednesday. How many more inches fell on Wednesday than Tuesday?
answer: 3 inches

subject: Reading
title: The Library Helper
passage: Ari noticed that the new student, Ben, stood alone near the library shelf. Everyone else was choosing books for the reading picnic. Ari wanted to grab the dragon book before someone else took it, but he stopped. He asked Ben what kinds of stories he liked. Together they found a mystery book and a funny animal book. Later, Ben sat with Ari and two other classmates. Ari did not get the dragon book, but he felt proud when Ben smiled during the picnic.

questions.prompt: What lesson does the story teach?
choices: Kindness can matter more than getting what you want.
New students do not like mysteries.
questions.answer: Kindness can matter more than getting what you want.

learning_target: I can describe a character using evidence.

subject: Reading
title: Why Bees Visit Flowers
passage: Bees fly from flower to flower looking for nectar. Nectar is a sweet liquid that gives bees energy. As bees move, yellow pollen sticks to their bodies. Some pollen rubs off on the next flower. This helps many plants make seeds and fruit. Bees get food, and plants get help. That is why bees and flowers are important to each other.

choices: Bees and flowers help each other.
questions.answer: Bees and flowers help each other.

questions.prompt: What do bees get from flowers?

learning_target: I can find the main idea and key details.

subject: Reading
title: The Missing Mittens
passage: Snow covered the playground when the class came inside. Mila reached into her coat sleeve, but one mitten was gone. She looked under the bench and inside her backpack. Then she saw tiny wet paw prints near the classroom pet cage. Inside the cage, the guinea pig was curled on something red and fuzzy.

choices: wet paw prints led to the cage
the bench was near the door
questions.answer: wet paw prints led to the cage

learning_target: I can use clues to make an inference.

subject: Reading
title: Two Ways to Help a Park
passage: Text A: Families picked up litter at Oak Park on Saturday. They filled twelve bags and found a lost soccer ball. Text B: Students planted native flowers at Oak Park. The flowers will give butterflies and bees a place to find food.

choices: Both tell about people helping a park.
questions.answer: Both tell about people helping a park.

choices: One removes litter; one adds plants.
One uses books; one uses bikes.
questions.answer: One removes litter; one adds plants.

questions.prompt: Which action might help pollinators most?

learning_target: I can compare ideas in two short texts.

subject: Science Reading
title: From Egg to Dragonfly
passage: A dragonfly begins life as an egg near water. A young dragonfly, called a nymph, lives underwater and eats tiny animals. After it grows, the nymph climbs out of the water. Its body changes, and an adult dragonfly flies away. Adult dragonflies lay eggs, and the cycle begins again.

questions.prompt: What happens after the nymph grows?
choices: It climbs out of the water and changes.
questions.answer: It climbs out of the water and changes.

questions.prompt: Why is this called a cycle?

learning_target: I can explain a life cycle.

subject: Science Reading
title: Desert Survival
passage: A desert is dry, but many living things survive there. Cactus plants store water in thick stems. Some animals sleep during the hot day and search for food at night. Lizards may have light-colored skin that helps them stay cooler. These traits help living things meet their needs in a dry habitat.

questions.prompt: Why do cactus plants have thick stems?
Why might animals search for food at night?
choices: It is cooler than during the day.
questions.answer: It is cooler than during the day.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mostly about?
choices: how living things survive in deserts
questions.answer: how living things survive in deserts

learning_target: I can connect traits to a habitat.

subject: Science Reading
title: Solids and Liquids Lab
passage: A solid keeps its shape unless something changes it. A liquid takes the shape of its container. In class, students put a cube, a marble, and water into different cups. The cube and marble kept their shapes. The water spread out to match each cup.

choices: takes the shape of its container
questions.answer: takes the shape of its container

learning_target: I can compare solids and liquids.

subject: Science Reading
title: Rocks Tell Clues
passage: Rocks can have different colors, textures, and patterns. Some rocks are smooth because water has rubbed them for a long time. Some rocks have tiny crystals that sparkle. Scientists observe rocks closely to learn where they may have formed and how they changed.

questions.prompt: What can make a rock smooth over time?

choices: to learn clues about how they formed and changed
to make every rock the same
questions.answer: to learn clues about how they formed and changed

learning_target: I can use properties to describe rocks.

subject: Writing
title: Best Recess Game
prompt: Write a paragraph explaining which recess game is best and why.
checklist: I gave at least two reasons.

subject: Writing
title: How to Care for a Class Plant
prompt: Explain how to care for a class plant using steps and details.
checklist: I explained why the plant needs care.

subject: Writing
title: The Surprise Sound
prompt: Write a story about a character who hears a surprising sound at school.
checklist: My story has a beginning, middle, and end.
I used details that help readers picture the scene.

subject: Writing
title: Letter to a Community Helper
prompt: Write a friendly letter thanking a community helper for their work.
checklist: I used a greeting and closing.
I explained why I am thankful.

subject: Social Studies
title: Map Symbols at the Zoo
passage: A map uses symbols to show places. A fork symbol can show food. A blue square can show restrooms. A dotted line can show a walking path. When visitors understand symbols, they can find places faster.

questions.prompt: What does a symbol do on a map?
choices: stands for a place or thing
questions.answer: stands for a place or thing

choices: They help people find places faster.
questions.answer: They help people find places faster.

learning_target: I can use map symbols.

subject: Social Studies
title: Bakery Choices
passage: A bakery sells bread, muffins, and cookies. These are goods because people can buy and take them home. The baker also decorates a birthday cake for a customer. That work is a service. Communities need both goods and services.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mostly about?

learning_target: I can tell goods and services apart.

subject: Social Studies
title: Schools Then and Now
passage: Long ago, some schools had one room for many grade levels. Students wrote on slates and often walked to school. Today, many schools have different classrooms, computers, buses, and libraries. Some things changed, but children still learn, read, and work together.

questions.prompt: What is one way schools changed?

choices: tell how things are alike and different
questions.answer: tell how things are alike and different

learning_target: I can compare past and present.

subject: Social Studies
title: The Playground Vote
passage: The class could choose one new playground item. Students listened to three ideas: a bench, a soccer net, or a garden box. Each student voted once. The teacher counted the votes and announced that the garden box won. Some students were disappointed, but they agreed to help plant it.

choices: helping even if your choice did not win
questions.answer: helping even if your choice did not win

learning_target: I can explain fair decision-making.

subject: Character
title: The Hard Puzzle
prompt: Maya says, “I am bad at puzzles.” What is a stronger thought?
choices: I can try a new strategy.
answer: I can try a new strategy.

subject: Character
title: Team Project Voices
prompt: Two classmates have different ideas for the poster. What should the team do?
choices: Listen to both ideas and combine the best parts.
answer: Listen to both ideas and combine the best parts.

subject: Character
title: Forgotten Homework Folder
prompt: You forgot your homework folder. What is the responsible next step?
choices: Tell the teacher and make a plan to fix it.
answer: Tell the teacher and make a plan to fix it.

subject: Character
title: Lunch Table Choice
prompt: A student is sitting alone at lunch. What shows empathy?
choices: Invite them to sit with you.
answer: Invite them to sit with you.

subject: Math
title: Trail-Mix Scaling Challenge
prompt: A hiking club needs 3 3/4 cups of oats for one trail-mix station. They are preparing 6 stations. They already have 18 cups of oats. How many more cups do they need?
answer: 4 1/2 cups
model_it: Convert 3 3/4 to 15/4. Six stations need 90/4 = 22 1/2 cups. 22 1/2 - 18 = 4 1/2.

subject: Math
title: Aquarium Design
prompt: A rectangular aquarium is 8 in. long, 5 in. wide, and 6 in. high. A decoration takes up 24 cubic inches. How much water can the tank hold around the decoration?
answer: 216 cubic inches
model_it: Tank volume is 8 × 5 × 6 = 240. Subtract 24 cubic inches for the decoration.

subject: Math
title: Seedling Growth Investigation
prompt: Six seedlings grew these amounts in inches: 1 1/4, 1 3/4, 2, 2, 2 1/2, 3. What is the total growth?
answer: 12 1/2 inches

subject: Math
title: Park Map Perimeter
prompt: A park rectangle has corners at (2,1), (9,1), (9,5), and (2,5). What is the perimeter?
answer: 22 units
model_it: Length is 9 - 2 = 7. Width is 5 - 1 = 4. Perimeter is 2(7 + 4) = 22.

subject: Reading
title: The Last Lantern
passage:
Every autumn, Mei’s family brought paper lanterns to the neighborhood festival. This year a basement leak ruined all but one lantern. Her uncle suggested buying new plastic ones, but Grandmother gently smoothed the wrinkled paper and said, “We can still keep the meaning.” Mei worried that one old lantern would look small beside the stage lights.

Before dusk, Grandmother told Mei about her first festival after moving to Ohio. She had felt lonely until a stranger recognized the song she was humming. Soon several families were singing together and sharing tea. “Traditions survive because people carry them together,” Grandmother said.

When the lantern finally glowed, visitors stopped to listen to Grandmother’s story. Mei realized the lantern mattered not because it was perfect, but because it connected memory to people standing together now.

choices: Traditions stay meaningful when people carry them together.
Old objects are always better than new objects.
Stage lights are more important than stories.
questions.answer: Traditions stay meaningful when people carry them together.
questions.evidence: Grandmother says traditions survive because people carry them together.

questions.prompt: Why does Mei change her mind?
choices: She sees people value the story behind the lantern.
questions.answer: She sees people value the story behind the lantern.

learning_target: I can explain theme using evidence.

subject: Reading
title: Why Wetlands Matter
passage:
A wetland is land covered with shallow water for all or part of the year. Although wetlands may look muddy and quiet, they are busy ecosystems. Birds nest in tall grasses, frogs lay eggs in still pools, and insects feed many animals.

Wetlands also help people. During heavy rain, wetland plants and soil slow the movement of water, which can reduce flooding. Wetlands act like natural filters too, trapping some dirt and pollution before water reaches streams or lakes.

For years, many people drained wetlands because they thought the land was useless. Today, communities restore wetlands because scientists understand their value. The author wants readers to see wetlands as living systems that protect both wildlife and people.

questions.prompt: What is the author’s main purpose?
choices: to explain why wetlands are valuable
to tell a fantasy story about frogs
to persuade readers to drain wetlands
questions.answer: to explain why wetlands are valuable

questions.prompt: Which detail shows wetlands help people?
choices: They slow water and reduce flooding.
questions.answer: They slow water and reduce flooding.

learning_target: I can identify purpose and supporting details.

subject: Reading
title: Two Views of the Storm
passage:
Marcus saw the dark clouds as a problem because soccer practice was finally getting exciting. His sister Talia, helping the coach, noticed different clues: birds flying low, a flag snapping hard, and thunder behind the school. To Marcus, the storm was an interruption. To Talia, it was a warning.

When the coach blew the whistle, Marcus wanted five more minutes. Talia guided younger players toward the gym. She remembered a storm that had flooded their apartment parking lot last spring. Marcus remembered only splashing afterward. As lightning flashed, Marcus finally understood why Talia had moved so quickly.

Later, Marcus wrote that Talia had read the weather clues better than he had. The same event felt different because each person brought different experience to it.

questions.prompt: How does Marcus view the storm at first?
How is Talia’s point of view different?
choices: She wants the game to continue.

questions.prompt: Why does the author include last spring’s flood?
choices: to show how past experience shapes Talia’s thinking
to explain that soccer is indoors
questions.answer: to show how past experience shapes Talia’s thinking

learning_target: I can compare points of view.

subject: Reading
title: Plastic in the Food Chain
passage:
Plastic is light, strong, and useful, but those qualities become problems when plastic is not disposed of correctly. A bottle dropped near a street drain can travel through streams and rivers. Over time, sunlight and waves can break plastic into tiny pieces called microplastics.

Small animals may mistake microplastics for food. Fish eat those animals, and larger predators eat the fish. In this way, pollution can move through a food chain even though plastic provides no nutrition.

The passage suggests that reducing pollution requires many actions. People can reduce waste, reuse items, recycle correctly, and support cleanup efforts. One person cannot clean an entire ocean, but one person can make choices that keep more plastic from reaching it.

choices: Trash dropped far from the ocean can still affect ocean animals.
questions.answer: Trash dropped far from the ocean can still affect ocean animals.

questions.prompt: Which evidence best supports that inference?
choices: A bottle can travel through drains, streams, and rivers.
Plastic is useful because it is light.
One person cannot clean an ocean.
questions.answer: A bottle can travel through drains, streams, and rivers.

choices: Small choices can either add to or reduce a large problem.
All plastic should be used once.
questions.answer: Small choices can either add to or reduce a large problem.

learning_target: I can make inferences with evidence.

subject: Science Reading
title: Separating a Mystery Mixture
passage: A class mixed sand, iron filings, salt, and water. At first the cup looked like muddy soup. The students planned a separation method based on properties. A magnet could remove the iron filings because iron is attracted to magnets. A filter could catch the sand because sand does not dissolve in water. The salt was harder to see because it dissolved evenly. To recover it, students could let the water evaporate, leaving salt crystals behind. Scientists choose separation methods by studying properties such as magnetism, particle size, and solubility.

questions.prompt: Which property helps remove iron filings?
Why does filtering remove sand but not salt?
choices: Sand does not dissolve, but salt dissolves in water.
questions.answer: Sand does not dissolve, but salt dissolves in water.

learning_target: I can connect material properties to a separation method.

subject: Science Reading
title: When Frogs Disappear
passage: In a meadow food web, grasses capture energy from sunlight. Insects feed on plants. Frogs eat many insects, while snakes and birds may eat frogs. If a disease reduces the frog population, the change does not stop with frogs. More insects may survive, which may lead to more plant damage. Animals that depend on frogs for food may struggle. Ecosystems are networks, so one population change can affect many others. Scientists collect data over time to understand these connections.

questions.prompt: What might happen if frogs decrease?
Why is a food web a network?
choices: Many organisms are connected in feeding relationships.
questions.answer: Many organisms are connected in feeding relationships.

questions.prompt: Why do scientists collect data over time?

learning_target: I can explain how ecosystem changes spread.

subject: Science Reading
title: Glaciers Shaped Ohio
passage: Long ago, huge sheets of ice moved across parts of Ohio. These glaciers scraped rock, carried soil, and left behind layers of sediment when they melted. In some places, glaciers flattened land. In others, they left hills, ridges, and kettle lakes. Scientists study landforms and sediments to infer where glaciers once traveled. The surface of Ohio is a record of slow, powerful Earth processes.

questions.prompt: What did glaciers leave behind when they melted?
What can scientists infer from landforms?
What is the passage mostly about?
choices: how to read a weather map

learning_target: I can use evidence to explain landform change.

subject: Science Reading
title: Circuit Troubleshooting
passage: A flashlight circuit needs a complete path from the battery through the bulb and back to the battery. If a wire is loose, the path is open and the bulb will not light. A switch works by opening or closing that path. Conductors, such as metal, allow electric current to move easily. Insulators, such as rubber, slow or block current. Engineers use conductors and insulators together to make devices that work safely. When a circuit fails, testing one part at a time helps locate the problem.

questions.prompt: What does a circuit need to light a bulb?

choices: opens or closes the circuit path
questions.answer: opens or closes the circuit path

questions.prompt: Why test one part at a time?

learning_target: I can explain circuits and troubleshooting.

subject: Writing
title: Should Schools Have a Weekly Design Lab?
prompt: Write an opinion essay arguing whether students should have a weekly design lab for building, testing, and improving ideas.
checklist: My conclusion connects to the claim.

subject: Writing
title: How Wetlands Protect Communities
prompt: Write an informative article explaining how wetlands support wildlife, reduce flooding, and filter water.

subject: Writing
title: The Failed Launch
prompt: Write a narrative about a team whose first design fails but leads to a better solution. Include dialogue and character growth.
checklist: My characters respond to a problem.

subject: Writing
title: Two Sources About Plastic Pollution
prompt: Use two source notes to write a response explaining how plastic pollution can move through waterways and food chains.
checklist: I combined information from two sources.
I used evidence, not just opinion.
I explained why the issue matters.

subject: Social Studies
title: Why Lake Erie Matters
passage: Lake Erie shapes life in northern Ohio. It provides drinking water, transportation routes, recreation, and habitat for fish and birds. However, because water from farms, cities, and streams can flow into the lake, pollution in one place can affect many communities. Citizens, scientists, and local governments monitor water quality and work to reduce runoff. A lake is not just a feature on a map; it is part of a region’s economy, environment, and daily life.

questions.prompt: Why does Lake Erie matter to communities?
choices: It provides water, transportation, recreation, and habitat.
It is only a decoration on maps.
It has no effect on people.
questions.answer: It provides water, transportation, recreation, and habitat.

questions.prompt: Why can pollution in one area affect others?
choices: Water flows into the lake from many places.
questions.answer: Water flows into the lake from many places.

questions.prompt: Which groups work on water quality?

learning_target: I can explain how geography affects communities.

subject: Social Studies
title: From Wheat to Bread
passage: Bread begins with natural resources such as soil, water, and wheat. Farmers use human resources, their labor and knowledge, to grow crops. They also use capital resources such as tractors, mills, ovens, and delivery trucks. At each stage, producers make choices about cost, quality, and demand. Trade connects farms, bakeries, stores, and families. Studying one loaf of bread shows how resources and markets work together.

questions.prompt: What does the bread example show?
choices: Only one person makes every product.

learning_target: I can classify resources and explain production.

subject: Social Studies
title: A Park Bench Proposal
passage: Students wanted a new bench near the playground. Instead of complaining, they gathered evidence. They counted how many students waited near the area, surveyed classmates, and wrote a respectful proposal to the principal and city parks office. The adults explained budget limits and safety rules. The students revised their plan to include a donated bench and a maintenance schedule. Civic participation often means asking questions, using evidence, and working toward a realistic solution.

questions.prompt: What did students do before making a proposal?
Why did they revise the plan?
choices: to respond to budget and safety limits
questions.answer: to respond to budget and safety limits

choices: taking informed action to improve a community
questions.answer: taking informed action to improve a community

learning_target: I can explain responsible civic participation.

subject: Social Studies
title: Reading a 1912 Letter
passage: A primary source is created during the time being studied. Imagine a 1912 letter from an Ohio child describing a new electric streetcar. The letter can show what the child noticed, how the ride felt, and what changed in daily life. However, it is only one person’s view. Historians compare letters, photographs, maps, and newspaper articles to build a fuller picture of the past.

questions.prompt: Why is the letter a primary source?
choices: It was written during the time being studied.
It is printed in a textbook only.
questions.answer: It was written during the time being studied.

choices: One source gives only one perspective.
questions.answer: One source gives only one perspective.

choices: how a child experienced the streetcar
questions.answer: how a child experienced the streetcar

learning_target: I can evaluate a primary source.

subject: Character
title: The Scoreboard Mistake
prompt: Your team receives points by mistake during a class game. No one notices except you. What shows integrity?
choices: Tell the teacher about the mistake.
Stay quiet because your team benefits.
answer: Tell the teacher about the mistake.

subject: Character
title: Group Chat Problem
prompt: A class group chat starts making jokes about a student’s presentation mistake. What is the best response?
choices: Ask them to stop and support the student.
Pretend kindness does not matter online.
answer: Ask them to stop and support the student.

subject: Character
title: Revision Frustration
prompt: Your essay draft gets many comments. What is the strongest next step?
choices: Choose two comments to improve first.
answer: Choose two comments to improve first.

subject: Character
title: Quiet Team Member
prompt: One teammate has not shared ideas during planning. What does a good leader do?
choices: Invite them to share and listen carefully.
Tell them their ideas are not needed.
answer: Invite them to share and listen carefully.

subject: Math
title: Solar Car Speed Trial
prompt: A solar car travels 18 meters in 3 seconds during Trial A and 35 meters in 5 seconds during Trial B. Which trial has the greater speed?
answer: Trial B
model_it: Find unit rates: Trial A is 18 ÷ 3 = 6 m/s. Trial B is 35 ÷ 5 = 7 m/s.

subject: Math
title: Museum Membership Equation
prompt: A museum charges $8 per visit or a $30 membership plus $3 per visit. After how many visits is the total cost the same?
answer: 6 visits

subject: Math
title: Which Center Fits?
prompt: Quiz scores are 72, 78, 80, 82, 83, 85, and 100. Which measure best represents a typical score if the high score is an outlier?
answer: median
model_it: The outlier 100 pulls the mean upward. The median is more resistant.

subject: Math
title: Submarine Depth Change
prompt: A submarine is at -120 feet. It rises 45 feet, then dives 70 feet. What is its final depth?
answer: -145 feet
model_it: Start at -120. Rising 45 gives -75. Diving 70 gives -145.

subject: Reading
title: Should Cities Darken the Night?
passage:
Many cities are reconsidering how they light streets, parks, and buildings at night. Supporters of “dark sky” policies argue that shielded lights and lower brightness can reduce energy use, help migrating birds, and make stars easier to see. They do not suggest removing all lights. Instead, they propose directing light downward and using brightness only where safety requires it.

Opponents worry that dimmer streets may feel less safe and that businesses depend on bright signs. They also argue that replacing fixtures costs money. Supporters respond that efficient lighting can save money over time and that careful design can protect safety while reducing waste.

The strongest arguments on both sides use evidence rather than emotion. A city deciding on a policy would need data about energy costs, accidents, wildlife, and public opinion.

questions.prompt: What is the author mostly explaining?
choices: the debate over dark sky lighting policies
how to remove all city lights
why businesses should close at night
questions.answer: the debate over dark sky lighting policies

choices: Shielded lights can reduce energy use and help wildlife.
questions.answer: Shielded lights can reduce energy use and help wildlife.

questions.prompt: What evidence would help a city decide?
choices: data about energy, safety, wildlife, and public opinion
the color of the mayor’s office
questions.answer: data about energy, safety, wildlife, and public opinion

learning_target: I can analyze claims and evidence.

subject: Reading
title: The Clockmaker’s Apprentice
passage:
Jonas wanted to repair the brass clock before the town exhibit opened. His first adjustment made the minute hand jump too quickly. His second made the bell ring at the wrong hour. Each mistake embarrassed him, especially because visitors could already see the display through the workshop window.

Master Elian did not take the tools away. Instead, he asked Jonas to record what each change had done. “A mistake that is measured becomes information,” he said. Jonas slowed down. He tested one gear at a time and wrote the result. By sunset, the clock ticked evenly.

At the exhibit, people praised the clock, but Jonas kept thinking about the notebook. The repair had taught him that patience was not waiting quietly; it was paying careful attention long enough to learn.

choices: Careful learning from mistakes can lead to mastery.
Tools are more important than thinking.
questions.answer: Careful learning from mistakes can lead to mastery.

questions.prompt: What does Master Elian teach Jonas?
What does Jonas realize about patience?
choices: It means paying careful attention long enough to learn.
questions.answer: It means paying careful attention long enough to learn.

learning_target: I can explain theme development.

subject: Reading
title: Two Views on School Gardens
passage:
Source A argues that school gardens improve science learning because students observe soil, insects, weather, and plant growth directly. It cites a class that used garden data to graph rainfall and plant height.

Source B argues that gardens build community because families, teachers, and students work together. It describes weekend planting days and donations from local businesses.

Both sources support school gardens, but they emphasize different benefits. A strong response should explain where the sources agree and how their evidence differs.

questions.prompt: How does Source A differ from Source B?
choices: A emphasizes science learning; B emphasizes community.
A is about sports; B is about music.
A opposes gardens; B supports them.
questions.answer: A emphasizes science learning; B emphasizes community.

questions.prompt: Which evidence belongs to Source A?
choices: students graph rainfall and plant height
questions.answer: students graph rainfall and plant height

learning_target: I can compare how sources support ideas.

subject: Reading
title: The Unseen Network
passage: Under a forest floor, fungi can connect with tree roots in a wide underground network. Through these connections, trees may exchange nutrients and chemical signals. Scientists are careful not to describe the network as magical or perfectly generous. The relationships are complex: fungi also take sugars from trees, and not every exchange helps every plant equally. Still, the network changes how researchers understand forests. A forest is not only a group of individual trees; it is a system of interactions.

questions.prompt: What does complex mean in the passage?
Why does the author warn against calling the network magical?
choices: The relationships are scientific and not always equally helpful.
questions.answer: The relationships are scientific and not always equally helpful.

questions.prompt: What idea does the passage emphasize?
choices: A forest is a system of interactions.
questions.answer: A forest is a system of interactions.

learning_target: I can use context to understand vocabulary and ideas.

subject: Science Reading
title: Why the Metal Spoon Feels Cold
passage: A metal spoon and a wooden spoon may sit in the same room at the same temperature, but the metal spoon often feels colder. That feeling happens because metal transfers thermal energy away from your hand faster than wood does. Your skin senses the quick energy loss as cold. Materials that transfer energy easily are conductors. Materials that transfer energy slowly are insulators. Engineers choose materials based on how quickly energy should move through them.

questions.prompt: Why does metal feel colder than wood at the same temperature?
choices: It transfers thermal energy from your hand faster.
questions.answer: It transfers thermal energy from your hand faster.

choices: a material that transfers energy easily
a material that blocks all light
questions.answer: a material that transfers energy easily

questions.prompt: Why do engineers choose materials carefully?
choices: Different materials transfer energy at different rates.
questions.answer: Different materials transfer energy at different rates.

learning_target: I can explain thermal energy transfer.

subject: Science Reading
title: Cells Working Together
passage: A single muscle cell can contract, but it cannot move an arm by itself. Many muscle cells form tissue. Tissues work with nerves, blood vessels, bones, and tendons as part of an organ system. The body depends on organization: cells make tissues, tissues help form organs, and organs work in systems. When one system changes, others may be affected. During exercise, muscles need more oxygen, so breathing and heart rate increase.

questions.prompt: What is the order of organization?
Why do heart rate and breathing increase during exercise?

choices: One cell moves an arm alone.

learning_target: I can explain levels of organization in living systems.

subject: Science Reading
title: A River Changes Its Banks
passage: After heavy rain, a river may run faster and carry more sediment. Fast-moving water can erode soil from the outside of a bend. When the water slows, it may deposit sediment on the inside of a bend or farther downstream. Over many years, these processes can change the shape of a river channel. Human choices, such as removing plants near banks, can increase erosion because roots help hold soil in place.

questions.prompt: Where can erosion be strongest in a bend?

learning_target: I can explain erosion and deposition.

subject: Science Reading
title: Models of the Solar System
passage: A model of the solar system can show the order of planets and the idea that planets orbit the sun. However, most classroom models cannot show true distances and sizes at the same time. If Earth were the size of a peppercorn, the distances between planets would be much larger than a poster can show. Models are useful because they highlight certain features, but they also have limits. Scientists choose or build models based on the question they are trying to answer.

questions.prompt: What is one limit of many classroom solar system models?
choices: They cannot show true sizes and distances at the same time.
They prove planets do not orbit.
questions.answer: They cannot show true sizes and distances at the same time.

choices: They highlight features of a system.
They make planets smaller in space.
questions.answer: They highlight features of a system.

questions.prompt: How should scientists choose a model?
choices: based on the question they are trying to answer
questions.answer: based on the question they are trying to answer

learning_target: I can evaluate scientific models.

subject: Writing
title: Should Cities Adopt Dark-Sky Lighting?
prompt: Write an argument using claims, evidence, reasoning, and a response to a counterclaim.
checklist: My claim is debatable and precise.
I explain how evidence supports my claim.

subject: Writing
title: How River Systems Change Land
prompt: Write an informative explanation of erosion, deposition, sediment, and how human choices affect riverbanks.

subject: Writing
title: Analyze Jonas’s Growth
prompt: Write a literary analysis paragraph explaining how Jonas changes in “The Clockmaker’s Apprentice.” Use two pieces of text evidence.
checklist: I make an interpretation, not just a summary.

subject: Writing
title: Two Sources, One Position
prompt: Use two source notes about school gardens to write a balanced response that compares benefits and recommends one action.

subject: Social Studies
title: Why Rivers Supported Early Civilizations
passage: Many early civilizations developed near rivers because rivers provided water, fertile soil, transportation, and food sources. The Nile, Tigris-Euphrates, Indus, and Huang He river valleys supported farming communities that could produce food surpluses. Surpluses allowed some people to specialize as builders, scribes, merchants, or leaders. Rivers also created challenges, including floods and conflicts over land and water. Geography did not determine everything, but it shaped the choices people made.

choices: They provided water and fertile soil.
questions.answer: They provided water and fertile soil.

choices: River geography created opportunities and challenges for early civilizations.
Rivers made all choices for people.
questions.answer: River geography created opportunities and challenges for early civilizations.

learning_target: I can explain how geography influenced civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
title: Direct and Representative Democracy
passage: In a direct democracy, citizens vote on laws or decisions themselves. In a representative democracy, citizens elect people to make decisions for them. Direct democracy can give citizens a strong voice, but it can be difficult when a population is large. Representative democracy can be more practical for large communities, but voters must stay informed and hold leaders accountable. Both systems connect to the question of how people participate in government.

questions.prompt: What happens in a representative democracy?
choices: Citizens elect people to make decisions.
Every citizen writes every law directly.
questions.answer: Citizens elect people to make decisions.

questions.prompt: Why can direct democracy be difficult for large populations?
choices: It is hard for everyone to vote on every decision.
questions.answer: It is hard for everyone to vote on every decision.

learning_target: I can compare forms of democracy.

subject: Social Studies
title: Specialization and Trade
passage: A region with rich farmland may specialize in growing grain. Another region with forests may specialize in timber. Specialization can make production more efficient, but regions then depend on trade to get goods they do not produce themselves. Trade networks connect people, spread ideas, and sometimes create conflict over prices, resources, or control of routes. Economic decisions are connected to geography, technology, and human wants.

choices: to get goods they do not produce themselves
questions.answer: to get goods they do not produce themselves

questions.prompt: What is one possible benefit of specialization?

learning_target: I can explain specialization and trade.

subject: Social Studies
title: Artifacts as Evidence
passage: An artifact is an object made or used by people in the past. A clay tablet, tool, coin, or piece of pottery can provide clues about technology, trade, beliefs, or daily life. Artifacts do not explain themselves. Historians and archaeologists ask where the object was found, what materials it uses, who might have used it, and what other evidence supports an interpretation. A single artifact can suggest possibilities, but stronger conclusions require multiple pieces of evidence.

choices: an object made or used by people in the past
questions.answer: an object made or used by people in the past

questions.prompt: Why ask where an artifact was found?
choices: It proves the artifact is fake.

questions.prompt: Why use multiple pieces of evidence?
choices: to make history simpler than it is

learning_target: I can evaluate artifacts as historical evidence.

subject: Character
title: AI Homework Decision
prompt: A tool gives you a complete answer to copy for homework. What shows academic integrity?
choices: Use it to understand ideas, then write your own response and cite help if required.
Submit it under a friend’s name.
answer: Use it to understand ideas, then write your own response and cite help if required.

subject: Character
title: Lab Partner Conflict
prompt: Your lab partner keeps changing the plan without asking. What is the most mature response?
choices: Calmly explain the problem and agree on roles.
answer: Calmly explain the problem and agree on roles.

subject: Character
title: Low Quiz Score
prompt: You studied but earned a low quiz score. What is the best next step?
choices: Review errors, ask for help, and plan targeted practice.
answer: Review errors, ask for help, and plan targeted practice.

subject: Character
title: Group Deadline
prompt: Your group is behind on a project. What should a responsible leader do first?
choices: Clarify tasks, set mini-deadlines, and check what help people need.
answer: Clarify tasks, set mini-deadlines, and check what help people need.
